title: A geometric view on Iwasawa theory
speaker: Mladen Dimitrov
abstract: We will investigate the geometry of the p-adic eigencurve at classical points where the Galois representation is locally trivial at p, and will give applications to Iwasawa and Hida theories.

proof_stages.label: Cyclotomic Iwasawa template and trivial zeros
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the Kubota-Leopoldt p-adic zeta function, the cyclotomic main conjecture, and the fact that trivial zeros require derivative formulas involving L-invariants rather than just characteristic ideals.

proof_stages.label: Regular submodules in motivic p-adic L-functions
proof_stages.summary: For a geometric p-adic Galois representation V, the Coates-Perrin-Riou framework attaches p-adic L-functions only after choosing a regular submodule D, and Benois-Greenberg trivial-zero formulas express derivatives using the associated L-invariant.

proof_stages.label: Eigenvariety variation as the geometric tool
proof_stages.summary: The key geometric idea is that the refined representation can itself vary p-adically in an eigenvariety, giving extra weight variables and enabling analytic continuation of p-adic L-functions even at motives with no critical values.

proof_stages.label: Locally scalar theta-series point with infinitely many regular lines
proof_stages.summary: The central case is a weight-one theta series from an imaginary quadratic field where p splits and the local Galois representation at p is scalar, so the possible regular submodules form a whole projective line.

proof_stages.label: Katz two-variable p-adic L-function and anti-cyclotomic prediction
proof_stages.summary: Katz's two-variable p-adic L-function for Hecke characters over the imaginary quadratic field is specialized to the relevant branch, where a Kronecker-limit-type formula gives a vanishing at the forbidden point and suggests a first-order anti-cyclotomic Taylor formula in terms of logarithmic L-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Four Hida components and four privileged p-adic lines
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem with Adel Betina proves nonvanishing statements via six exponentials, identifies the anti-cyclotomic first derivative, and shows that, under the stated nonvanishing hypothesis, exactly four smooth Hida families pass through the theta point, yielding four distinguished G_Qp-stable lines.

proof_stages.label: Failure of a global ordinary basis
proof_stages.summary: The four different residual ordinary lines imply that the big Galois representation over the completed local eigencurve is generically ordinary on components but not ordinary over the whole local algebra.

proof_stages.label: Classification of known local eigencurve geometries
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by situating the theorem among known cases of local eigencurve geometry at classical points, comparing cohomological and weight-one, regular and irregular, CM, RM, Eisenstein, and exotic cases, and identifying the remaining open geometries.

examples.name: Kubota-Leopoldt Dirichlet-character trivial zero
examples.note: The odd Dirichlet-character p-adic zeta function supplies the motivating template in which an Euler factor forces a trivial zero and a derivative is expected to equal a classical value times an L-invariant.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sets up the Dirichlet-character Kubota-Leopoldt p-adic zeta function, writes its interpolation factor, identifies when the trivial zero occurs, and discusses its order and derivative formula

examples.name: Elliptic curve with multiplicative reduction at p
examples.note: This gives a concrete instance of the 'unique' case, where the regular submodule is forced and the resulting L-invariant is the Fontaine-Mazur one used in the Mazur-Tate-Teitelbaum setting.
examples.engagement_reason: the elliptic curve with multiplicative reduction is cited as an instance of the unique regular-submodule case, with facts stated but no concrete calculation on the curve

passes.reason: Multiplicative reduction is a singular degeneration of good reduction and has maximal monodromy rank in the stated unique case.
Multiplicative reduction gives a nodal bad special fiber and, in the associated semistable representation, maximal monodromy rank.
Multiplicative reduction is a nodal degeneration and in the talk gives the maximal-monodromy unique-submodule case.
multiplicative reduction is a nodal semistable degeneration with maximal monodromy rank giving the unique regular submodule.
Multiplicative reduction has a singular special fiber and gives the maximal-rank monodromy case, used only to illustrate the unique regular submodule.
multiplicative reduction is a semistable nodal degeneration and gives maximal monodromy/unique regular submodule.
multiplicative reduction gives a nodal semistable degeneration with maximal possible monodromy rank in the two-dimensional elliptic-curve representation.

multiplicative reduction gives a nodal/bad special fiber and, as the speaker notes, maximal monodromy rank giving a unique regular submodule

multiplicative reduction is a semistable degeneration and its monodromy has maximal rank, serving as the unique-regular-submodule case

examples.name: Eisenstein and Hida families as early p-adic families
examples.note: The Eisenstein family and Hida's ordinary cuspform families illustrate the general principle that the motive or refinement can vary p-adically in a weight direction.
examples.engagement_reason: Eisenstein and Hida families are invoked historically as early p-adic families, without running any construction or computation on a specific family

examples.name: Weight-one eigenform with two distinct p-stabilizations
examples.note: A weight-one Artin modular form with distinct roots alpha and beta illustrates how eigenvariety variation constructs p-adic L-functions even when the original motive has no critical values.
examples.engagement_reason: for a weight-one eigenform with distinct Hecke roots, the speaker applies the p-stabilization/refinement construction and explicitly writes the stabilization formula for f_alpha

passes.reason: A weight-one form lies at the boundary of the usual cohomological-weight range, with distinct p-stabilizations used as a positive application.

examples.name: Even Artin representations and p-adic rigidity
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker describes even Artin representations as p-adically rigid by a six-exponential argument, contrasting them with the odd weight-one representations considered in the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: even Artin representations are discussed in the questions only by stating a rigidity result via the six-exponential theorem, without showing the argument or working through an example

passes.reason: Even Artin representations are described as p-adically rigid with a collapsed deformation space, obstructing the hoped-for deformation picture.
The even Artin representations are used to show hoped-for p-adic deformations cannot exist, with rigidity collapsing the deformation space to an isolated point.
